--- Page 1 ---
DriftSched: Adaptive QoS-Aware Scheduling under
Runtime Token Drift for Multi-Tenant GPU
Inference
Kathiravan Palaniappan
Independent Researcher (University of Colorado Colorado Springs Alumni)
kpalania@uccs.edu
Abstract—The rapid growth of large language model (LLM)
inference services has increased the demand for efficient multi-
tenant GPU scheduling in modern AI datacenters. Recent
inference-serving frameworks such as Orca, Nexus, Sarathi,
FlexGen, and vLLM have demonstrated significant advances
in throughput optimization, memory management, and large-
scale LLM serving [16], [17], [19]–[21]. While inference runtimes
such as vLLM improve throughput through continuous batching
and optimized memory management, accurately estimating the
runtime cost of heterogeneous inference requests remains a major
challenge. Existing scheduling approaches often rely on static
token limits, user-specified output constraints, or predefined
workload assumptions that fail to capture the dynamic behavior
of real-world LLM inference workloads. In practice, observed
output lengths frequently deviate from expected values due to
variations in prompt complexity, response characteristics, model
behavior, and workload composition. This phenomenon, referred
to as runtime token drift, introduces estimation errors that can
propagate into scheduling decisions, leading to workload mis-
classification, queue imbalance, inflated tail latency, unfair tenant
resource allocation, and degraded Quality-of-Service (QoS) under
contention.
This paper presents DriftSched, an adaptive QoS-aware
scheduling framework for multi-tenant LLM inference serving
on NVIDIA L4 GPUs. The proposed system combines work-
load classification, token-budget estimation, tenant-aware queue
management, and runtime feedback-driven drift compensation to
dynamically estimate inference cost prior to GPU execution. In-
ference requests are grouped into Premium, Standard, and Batch
service tiers and evaluated under multiple scheduling strate-
gies including FIFO, Priority Scheduling, Weighted Scheduling,
Shortest-Job-First (SJF), and Aging Priority Scheduling. Unlike
static scheduling approaches, DriftSched continuously compares
estimated token budgets and observed output lengths collected
after inference execution and adaptively updates workload-
specific bias factors to compensate for runtime token drift and
improve future admission-time scheduling decisions.
Experimental evaluation is conducted under heterogeneous
multi-tenant inference workloads with varying prompt complex-
ity, output length, arrival rates, and tenant priority under GPU
saturation scenarios. Results demonstrate the existence of mea-
surable runtime token drift across workload categories, where
static token-budget estimation consistently overestimates actual
generation behavior. Across five scheduling policies, the proposed
adaptive feedback mechanism reduced workload estimation error
by an average of 38.8% (MAE) and 40.5% (RMSE), improving
workload classification stability and admission-time scheduling
accuracy. The evaluation further shows that scheduling policy
selection significantly influences fairness, queue dynamics, tenant
QoS, and tail-latency behavior. Among all evaluated schedulers,
SJF achieved the best overall performance, reducing median
end-to-end latency by approximately 42% and P99 latency by
approximately 16% relative to FIFO scheduling under sustained
GPU contention.
This work contributes an adaptive drift-aware scheduling
architecture, a runtime token-drift compensation mechanism, and
a reproducible benchmarking framework for evaluating QoS-
aware LLM inference scheduling on shared GPU infrastructure.
The findings provide practical insights for enterprise AI serving
platforms and future adaptive inference runtimes operating
under multi-tenant GPU contention.
Index Terms—GPU Scheduling, QoS-Aware Scheduling, Multi-
Tenant Inference, NVIDIA L4, LLM Inference, Tail Latency,
GPU Utilization, AI Infrastructure, Datacenter GPUs, Inference
Serving
I. INTRODUCTION
The rapid adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) has
significantly increased the demand for efficient GPU inference
serving in modern AI datacenters. The emergence of large-
scale foundation models such as GPT-3 demonstrated the
effectiveness of scaling model parameters and training data
to achieve strong zero-shot and few-shot reasoning capabil-
ities across a wide range of natural language tasks
[7].
Enterprise AI platforms, cloud inference providers, and edge
AI systems increasingly operate in multi-tenant environments
where heterogeneous inference requests compete for shared
GPU resources. These workloads often exhibit substantial
variability in prompt complexity, generated output size, latency
sensitivity, and Quality-of-Service (QoS) requirements. While
modern inference runtimes such as vLLM improve throughput
through continuous batching and optimized memory manage-
ment, efficient scheduling of concurrent inference requests re-
mains a major challenge under GPU contention. Modern Large
Language Models (LLMs) are built upon the Transformer
architecture, which introduced self-attention mechanisms ca-
pable of modeling long-range dependencies while enabling
highly parallelized training and inference workflows [6].
While prior work such as GDEV-AI [1] demonstrated
that modern CPU architectures have significantly improved
deep learning inference performance for offline analytics,
background processing, and non-latency-critical AI workloads,
real-time large-scale LLM inference serving continues to rely
heavily on GPU accelerators due to their superior parallel
execution capability and high-throughput tensor processing.
As CPUs increasingly absorb portions of asynchronous and
arXiv:2606.02982v1  [cs.PF]  2 Jun 2026


--- Page 2 ---
non-critical inference workloads, GPUs are becoming more
specialized toward latency-sensitive, real-time multi-tenant
inference systems where efficient scheduling, fairness, and
Quality-of-Service (QoS) management are critical under con-
tention.
Unlike traditional offline inference environments where
workloads can be processed in bulk or asynchronously, multi-
tenant LLM serving platforms must dynamically balance het-
erogeneous inference requests with varying prompt complex-
ity, observed output lengths, latency sensitivity, and tenant
priority. Under GPU saturation, inefficient workload estima-
tion and scheduling can lead to queue imbalance, starvation
of smaller requests, increased tail latency [8], and degraded
QoS for premium tenants. These challenges motivate the need
for adaptive runtime-aware scheduling frameworks capable
of improving workload estimation and resource allocation in
modern GPU inference infrastructure.
Furthermore, even as portions of offline and non-critical
inference workloads are increasingly shifted toward modern
CPU platforms, selecting the appropriate inference-optimized
GPU architecture for latency-sensitive LLM serving workloads
remains an important challenge. Prior work such as DEEP-
GAP [2] evaluated the architectural behavior of modern infer-
ence GPUs including the NVIDIA T4
[5] and NVIDIA L4
[4] under deep learning inference workloads, demonstrating
substantial differences in throughput scaling, latency behavior,
execution parallelism, and efficiency across precision modes
and workload sizes. The study highlighted that modern infer-
ence accelerators continue to play a critical role in real-time
AI serving environments where low latency, high concurrency,
and efficient GPU utilization are essential.
However, while GPU architectural studies provide insights
into raw inference performance characteristics, they do not
fully address how concurrent multi-tenant inference requests
should be scheduled under contention in practical serving
environments. In real-world LLM serving systems, workload
behavior is heavily influenced not only by GPU architectural
capability, but also by runtime scheduling policies, queue
management strategies, workload estimation accuracy, tenant
prioritization, and batching dynamics. As a result, efficient
QoS-aware scheduling remains a fundamental challenge even
on highly optimized inference GPU platforms such as the
NVIDIA L4.
A key difficulty in LLM inference scheduling is accu-
rately estimating the runtime cost of inference requests be-
fore execution. Many existing systems rely on user-defined
max_tokens values or static workload assumptions to ap-
proximate inference cost. However, the actual generated out-
put length frequently differs from the predicted or allocated
token budget. This mismatch between predicted and actual
generation behavior introduces a phenomenon referred to in
this work as runtime token drift. Runtime token drift occurs
when workload-specific token generation characteristics devi-
ate from admission-time estimates due to prompt variability,
response complexity, model behavior, and runtime execution
dynamics. Under multi-tenant GPU contention, such estima-
tion errors can propagate into scheduling decisions, resulting
in workload misclassification, queue imbalance, increased tail
latency, and degraded Quality-of-Service (QoS).
Misclassification of workload size can negatively impact
fairness, latency, and resource utilization during contention.
Overestimation of token demand may cause short latency-
sensitive requests to be incorrectly classified as long-running
workloads, resulting in unnecessary queue delays and under-
utilization of GPU resources. Conversely, underestimation of
runtime cost may allow computationally expensive requests to
enter low-latency queues, causing queue imbalance, starvation
of smaller requests, increased tail latency, and QoS degradation
for premium tenants. Under high concurrency, these effects
become amplified as scheduling policies attempt to balance
throughput, fairness, and latency objectives simultaneously.
Traditional scheduling algorithms such as First-Come First-
Serve (FIFO), Priority Scheduling, and Shortest-Job-First
(SJF) [9], [10] were originally designed for CPU and dis-
tributed systems workloads where execution cost is relatively
predictable. In modern LLM inference systems, however, run-
time behavior depends on several dynamic factors including
prompt length, observed output length, KV-cache growth,
batching behavior, tenant priority, and GPU memory con-
tention. As a result, static scheduling policies alone are often
insufficient for maintaining fairness and predictable latency in
multi-tenant inference environments.
To address these challenges, this paper presents an adap-
tive QoS-aware scheduling framework for multi-tenant GPU
inference serving on NVIDIA L4 GPUs. The proposed system
introduces a workload analysis layer that combines token-
budget estimation, workload classification, and tenant-aware
queue management to estimate inference cost before GPU
execution. Inference requests are grouped into Premium, Stan-
dard, and Batch service tiers and evaluated under multiple
scheduling strategies including FIFO, Priority Scheduling,
Weighted Scheduling, Shortest-Job-First (SJF), and Aging
Priority Scheduling.
Unlike static scheduling approaches that rely solely on
predefined token limits, DriftSched incorporates a runtime
feedback mechanism that continuously compares estimated
token budgets against observed output lengths collected during
inference execution. The framework treats workload estima-
tion as a runtime token drift compensation problem and adap-
tively updates workload-specific bias factors using execution
feedback. This feedback-driven adaptation improves future
admission-time scheduling decisions by reducing estimation
error and increasing workload classification stability under
sustained multi-tenant inference traffic.
This work contributes an adaptive workload-aware schedul-
ing architecture for multi-tenant LLM inference serving, a
feedback-driven token-budget estimation mechanism, and a
comparative evaluation of multiple scheduling strategies un-
der heterogeneous GPU inference workloads. In addition,
the study introduces a reproducible benchmarking framework
capable of simulating concurrent multi-tenant inference traf-
fic under GPU saturation scenarios. Experimental evaluation


--- Page 3 ---
analyzes queue wait time, latency, fairness, throughput, GPU
utilization, and workload prediction accuracy on NVIDIA L4
GPUs using vLLM-based inference execution.
The findings of this study provide practical insights into
adaptive QoS-aware scheduling for modern AI inference in-
frastructure and highlight the importance of runtime-aware
workload estimation for future multi-tenant GPU serving sys-
tems.
A. Related Work
Recent LLM serving systems have focused on improv-
ing throughput, memory utilization, and scalability. Nexus
introduced scalable GPU cluster scheduling for inference
workloads [19]. Orca proposed iteration-level scheduling and
continuous batching mechanisms that improve accelerator uti-
lization for generative models [16]. Sarathi further improved
inference efficiency through chunked-prefill execution and
optimized handling of prefill and decode phases [17]. FlexGen
explored high-throughput LLM serving using heterogeneous
hardware resources and memory offloading strategies [21].
More recently, vLLM introduced PagedAttention and efficient
KV-cache management techniques that significantly improve
LLM serving throughput [20].
FastServing explored low-latency distributed inference serv-
ing for deep learning workloads and highlighted the impor-
tance of efficient request dispatching and scalable serving
architectures for production AI systems [18].
Recent research has also investigated fairness and isola-
tion mechanisms in multi-tenant LLM serving environments,
highlighting the challenges of balancing tenant QoS, resource
sharing, and workload interference under shared GPU infras-
tructure.
Unlike these systems, DriftSched focuses on runtime to-
ken drift compensation and adaptive workload estimation for
improving admission-time scheduling decisions under multi-
tenant GPU contention.
II. METHODOLOGY AND SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE
This section describes the proposed adaptive QoS-aware
scheduling framework for multi-tenant LLM inference serving
on NVIDIA L4 GPUs. The framework was designed to
study how workload estimation accuracy, queue management
policies, and scheduling algorithms influence fairness, latency,
throughput, and GPU utilization under concurrent inference
contention. The complete architecture consists of workload
generation, workload analysis, tenant-aware queue manage-
ment, scheduling engines, GPU inference execution, runtime
metrics collection, and adaptive feedback-driven workload
estimation.
Figure 1 illustrates the overall architecture of the proposed
framework.
A. Experimental Workflow
The experimental workflow begins with the workload gen-
eration layer, which produces heterogeneous inference traffic
representing multiple tenants and workload categories. Gen-
erated requests are submitted to the API Gateway, where
the workload analysis layer estimates inference cost, classi-
fies workload size, and assigns requests into tenant-specific
queues. Scheduling policies then determine the order in which
requests are dispatched to the GPU inference worker. Runtime
metrics collected during inference execution are continuously
analyzed to detect and compensate for runtime token drift.
The adaptive feedback mechanism compares estimated token
budgets against observed output lengths and updates workload-
specific bias factors used by future admission decisions.
This feedback-driven adaptation improves workload classifi-
cation stability and reduces estimation error under hetero-
geneous multi-tenant inference workloads. The API Gate-
way component was implemented using FastAPI, providing
lightweight REST-based request submission and integration
between workload generation, queue management, and in-
ference execution services
[12]. The framework supports
concurrent inference execution under GPU saturation condi-
tions using vLLM-based inference serving on NVIDIA L4
GPUs. Experimental evaluation compares multiple scheduling
algorithms under mixed workload contention scenarios.
B. Workload Generation
The workload generator produces concurrent inference re-
quests with heterogeneous prompt complexity and tenant pri-
ority levels. Requests are generated using multiple workload
categories including short question-answer prompts, summa-
rization workloads, technical explanations, and long-form re-
port generation tasks. The workload generator uses weighted
probabilistic selection to simulate realistic traffic distributions
[9] observed in enterprise AI serving environments.
The workload generation framework supports configurable
request volume and concurrency levels. Concurrent request
submission is implemented using Python thread pools to
generate burst traffic conditions capable of saturating GPU
inference queues. Workloads are generated for three tenant
service tiers:
• Premium tenants representing latency-sensitive work-
loads.
• Standard tenants representing balanced QoS workloads.
• Batch tenants representing throughput-oriented back-
ground inference workloads.
Algorithm 1 illustrates the workload generation process
used to simulate heterogeneous multi-tenant inference traffic.
The framework randomly selects prompt categories using
weighted probability distributions to emulate heterogeneous
multi-tenant traffic patterns.
C. Workload Analysis Layer
The primary objective of the workload analysis layer is to
estimate inference cost prior to execution while compensating
for runtime token drift. Runtime token drift refers to the de-
viation between predicted token budgets and observed output
lengths observed during inference execution. Since scheduling
decisions depend on workload size estimates, inaccurate token


--- Page 4 ---
Fig. 1: Proposed adaptive QoS-aware multi-tenant LLM inference architecture. Incoming requests are classified using adaptive
token-cost estimation and mapped to tenant-specific queues. Multiple scheduling policies (FIFO, Priority Scheduling, Weighted,
SJF, and Aging Priority) dispatch requests to the GPU inference worker running vLLM. Runtime metrics are analyzed through a
bias-correction feedback loop that continuously refines token-cost estimation based on estimated token budgets versus observed
output lengths.
Algorithm 1 Workload Generation Process
1: Define workload categories:
2:
short qa, summary, technical, report
3: Define weighted probabilities for each category
4: for each incoming request do
5:
Randomly select workload category using weighted
distribution
6:
Assign tenant tier:
7:
Premium, Standard, or Batch
8:
Generate prompt from selected category
9:
Submit request to API Gateway
10: end for
predictions may lead to workload misclassification, queue
imbalance, increased latency, and degraded QoS under GPU
contention. The workload analysis layer performs inference
cost estimation and workload classification before requests
enter scheduling queues. This layer consists of two major
components:
1) Adaptive Token Estimator
2) Workload Classifier
1) Token Budget Estimation: A major challenge in LLM in-
ference scheduling is estimating runtime cost before inference
execution begins. Many production inference systems rely
on static max_tokens limits configured by users. However,
actual generated output length can vary significantly depending
on prompt complexity and model behavior, creating estima-
tion inaccuracies that negatively affect scheduling decisions.
Similar challenges have been observed in predictive resource
allocation systems, where inaccurate predictions may lead
to workload misclassification. However, the observed output
length often differs substantially from the configured upper
bound. This mismatch introduces a phenomenon referred to in
this work as runtime token drift, where the observed token gen-
eration behavior deviates from admission-time estimates due to
prompt variability, response complexity, model behavior, and
runtime execution dynamics. Since scheduling decisions are
frequently based on estimated workload size, runtime token
drift can lead to workload misclassification, queue imbalance,
increased tail latency, and degraded Quality-of-Service (QoS)
under GPU contention.
Observed output length was measured using whitespace-
delimited word counts obtained from generated responses.
Although this metric does not represent exact tokenizer token
counts, it provides a consistent and computationally inexpen-
sive proxy for generated output volume across all experiments.
To compensate for runtime token drift, the proposed
DriftSched framework employs an adaptive token-budget esti-


--- Page 5 ---
Fig. 2: Example workload misclassification caused by runtime
token drift. Predicted workload classes may differ from actual
runtime classes observed during GPU execution. Underesti-
mation occurs when a request generates more tokens than
expected, while overestimation occurs when actual runtime
cost is lower than predicted.
mation mechanism. The estimator combines workload classi-
fication, tenant-aware safety factors, runtime feedback-driven
bias correction, and input prompt complexity to estimate
expected output token count prior to inference execution.
Unlike static approaches that rely solely on predefined token
limits, the proposed estimator continuously adapts workload-
specific bias values using execution feedback collected during
runtime.
The estimated workload budget is computed as:
Tbudget = Tinput + Testimated output
(1)
The estimated output token count is calculated using:
Testimated output = Tbase × Bruntime
× Stenant × Finput
(2)
where:
• Tbase represents the baseline workload token estimate.
• Bruntime represents the adaptive runtime drift-correction
factor learned from previous inference executions.
• Stenant represents tenant-aware safety scaling.
• Finput represents prompt complexity scaling.
The adaptive bias factor Bruntime is continuously updated
using runtime feedback collected after inference completion.
By comparing estimated token budgets against observed output
lengths, DriftSched compensates for runtime token drift and
improves future workload estimation accuracy. This adaptive
mechanism enables the scheduler to make more informed
admission-time decisions while maintaining compatibility with
multiple queue management policies. Observed output length
was measured using whitespace-delimited word counts ob-
tained from generated model responses. While this metric does
not represent exact tokenizer token counts, it provides a con-
sistent and computationally inexpensive proxy for generated
output volume across all experiments.
Algorithm 2 illustrates the adaptive token-budget estimation
process.
Algorithm 2 Adaptive Token Budget Estimation with Runtime
Drift Compensation
1: Classify workload category
2: Retrieve baseline token estimate
3: Retrieve adaptive runtime bias factor
4: Determine tenant safety factor
5: Compute prompt complexity factor
6: Compute estimated output tokens:
7: Testimated output = Tbase × Bruntime × Stenant × Finput
8: Return estimated token budget
2) Workload Classification: After token-budget estimation,
requests are classified into three workload categories: short,
medium, and long. Classification is based on the estimated
total token budget:
short ≤128,
128 < medium ≤512,
long > 512
(3)
The workload classifier enables scheduling policies such as
SJF to prioritize smaller inference requests while identifying
potentially long-running workloads that may impact fairness
and latency. Because runtime token drift may cause observed
output lengths to deviate from admission-time estimates, work-
load classification is continuously refined through the adaptive
runtime feedback mechanism described in Section II-C. This
allows runtime scheduling classes to evolve as workload-
specific token generation behavior becomes better understood.
D. Workload Category and Runtime Job Classification
The proposed framework separates semantic prompt clas-
sification from runtime scheduling classification. Prompt cat-
egories represent the logical intent of the inference request,
while runtime job types represent estimated computational cost
used by the scheduler.
The workload classifier initially categorizes prompts into
one of four semantic workload categories:
• short_qa
• summary
• technical
• report
Each semantic category is associated with a baseline token
estimate used by the adaptive token estimator. The estimated
runtime cost is then mapped into scheduling-oriented job
classes:
short,
medium,
long


--- Page 6 ---
based on the estimated total token budget.
The runtime scheduling classification is computed as:
job_type =





short,
Tbudget ≤128
medium,
128 < Tbudget ≤512
long,
Tbudget > 512
(4)
In the current implementation, workloads categorized as
short_qa typically map to short runtime jobs, while
summary and technical workloads commonly map to
medium runtime jobs. Long-form report workloads typi-
cally map to long runtime jobs due to their larger estimated
token budgets.
This separation between semantic workload category and
runtime scheduling classification enables the framework to
decouple prompt intent from estimated computational cost, al-
lowing scheduling policies to operate using estimated runtime
complexity rather than prompt semantics alone.
For example, prompts categorized as technical may
exhibit significantly different runtime behavior depending on
prompt length, expected output size, and adaptive runtime
estimation feedback. As a result, a semantic workload category
does not always map to a fixed runtime scheduling class.
A technically-oriented prompt with limited expected output
may be classified as a medium runtime workload, while a
longer technical explanation request may be classified as a
long runtime workload. Similarly, summarization tasks may
shift between short and medium runtime classifications de-
pending on token-budget estimation and adaptive runtime bias
correction.
This distinction is important because scheduling policies
operate on estimated computational cost rather than semantic
intent alone. Runtime scheduling decisions therefore depend
on dynamically estimated token budgets rather than static
prompt categories.
The framework records both semantic workload category
and runtime scheduling class within the metrics pipeline to
enable comparative analysis between logical workload intent
and observed runtime behavior during scheduling evaluation.
TABLE I: Example Mapping Between Semantic Workload
Categories and Runtime Scheduling Classes
Semantic Cate-
gory
Typical
Runtime Class
Example Prompt
short_qa
short
“What is DNS?”
summary
medium
“Summarize
how
Kubernetes schedules
pods.”
technical
medium /
long
“Explain
distributed
systems
consistency
and retries.”
report
medium /
long
“Write a detailed re-
port on network out-
age summarizing af-
fected services.”
E. Tenant Queue Management
After workload analysis, requests are grouped into tenant-
specific service queues managed by the tenant queue manager.
Three independent queues are maintained:
• Premium Queue
• Standard Queue
• Batch Queue
Each queue stores heterogeneous workloads consisting of
short, medium, and long inference requests. Tenant isolation
allows scheduling policies to enforce QoS differentiation un-
der concurrent contention. In DriftSched, queue assignment
decisions depend on workload classifications generated by the
adaptive token estimation layer. Consequently, improvements
in runtime token drift compensation directly influence queue
composition and scheduling behavior.
The queue manager supports multiple queue implementa-
tions using Redis-based distributed data structures [11]. FIFO
scheduling uses Redis lists to preserve request arrival order.
Priority, Weighted, and Aging Priority schedulers maintain
separate tenant-specific queues and apply scheduling poli-
cies during request selection. SJF scheduling incorporates
workload-size information to prioritize shorter estimated work-
loads.
F. Scheduling Engine
The scheduling engine determines the order in which
queued inference requests are dispatched to the GPU inference
worker. Multiple scheduling strategies were implemented to
evaluate fairness, latency behavior, starvation characteristics,
and throughput under contention.
The evaluated scheduling policies are inspired by traditional
operating system scheduling techniques and large-scale dis-
tributed scheduling systems such as Sparrow [15], as well as
modern multi-tenant accelerator scheduling architectures [19].
The objective is to study how adaptive workload estimation
affects queue behavior and tenant QoS under GPU saturation.
G. Calibration and Evaluation Phases
To evaluate adaptive runtime token drift compensation under
sustained load, each experiment was divided into two phases:
a calibration phase and a stress evaluation phase. A total of
3000 inference requests were generated for each experimental
run.
The first 1000 requests were used as a calibration phase,
during which runtime feedback was collected and workload-
specific bias factors were continuously updated. This phase
allowed the adaptive estimator to observe workload behavior,
compensate for runtime token drift, and gradually converge
toward stable workload-specific token estimates.
The remaining 2000 requests were used as the stress eval-
uation phase. During this phase, the scheduler operated using
the bias values learned during calibration while continuing
to receive runtime feedback and perform online adaptation.
This approach ensured that scheduling decisions during the
majority of the experiment benefited from previously observed


--- Page 7 ---
workload behavior while still allowing the estimator to respond
to changing workload characteristics.
The selected 1:2 calibration-to-evaluation ratio provides a
balance between estimator learning and scheduler evaluation.
A calibration phase that is too short may not provide sufficient
runtime observations for bias convergence, while an exces-
sively long calibration phase reduces the number of requests
available for scheduling evaluation. The chosen configuration
allows the adaptive estimator to establish stable workload-
specific bias values before the system enters sustained high-
load operation.
Unlike offline training approaches, DriftSched performs
continuous online adaptation throughout execution. Runtime
token drift compensation therefore occurs within the same ex-
perimental cycle used for scheduling evaluation. As additional
requests complete, newly observed token-generation behavior
is incorporated into future workload estimates through adap-
tive bias updates. This enables the scheduler to progressively
refine workload classification and admission-time scheduling
decisions while the workload is actively executing.
1) FIFO Scheduling: FIFO scheduling dispatches requests
strictly in arrival order
[10] without considering workload
size or tenant priority. While FIFO provides fairness in terms
of arrival ordering, long-running requests may block smaller
latency-sensitive requests under burst traffic conditions.
2) Priority Scheduling: Priority Scheduling assigns higher
execution precedence to Premium tenants while preserving
FIFO ordering within each priority tier. Separate queues are
maintained for Premium, Standard, and Batch tenants. During
scheduling, requests are selected from the highest-priority
non-empty queue, ensuring that Premium workloads receive
preferential access to GPU resources. Requests within the
same tenant tier are processed in arrival order using FIFO
semantics.
The scheduling score is computed as:
score = (priority_score × 1012) + arrival_time
This mechanism ensures:
• Premium requests execute before Standard requests.
• Standard requests execute before Batch requests.
• FIFO ordering is preserved within each priority tier.
3) Shortest-Job-First Scheduling: SJF scheduling [9], [10]
prioritizes requests with lower estimated token budgets. The
scheduler prioritizes requests with smaller estimated token
budgets produced by the adaptive token estimation layer. As
a result, SJF is particularly sensitive to token estimation ac-
curacy and provides a useful evaluation platform for studying
the impact of runtime token drift on scheduling behavior.
4) Weighted Scheduling: Weighted scheduling [15] parti-
tions GPU service capacity across tenant classes using pre-
defined execution ratios. The proposed implementation uses
a:
50/30/20
Fig. 3: DriftSched adaptive runtime learning mechanism. Run-
time token drift is detected by comparing estimated token
budgets and observed output lengths during GPU execution.
The observed prediction error is used to update workload-
specific bias factors, which are subsequently fed back into
the workload estimation layer to improve future scheduling
decisions.
distribution for:
Premium : Standard : Batch
requests respectively. The scheduler cyclically dispatches re-
quests from tenant-specific queues according to the configured
ratio.
5) Aging Priority Scheduling: To mitigate starvation [10],
aging priority scheduling dynamically increases request prior-
ity as queue waiting time grows. Waiting time progressively
reduces the effective scheduling score, allowing long-waiting
requests to eventually execute even under continuous high-
priority traffic.
H. GPU Inference Execution
Inference execution is performed using vLLM [3], [20]
running on NVIDIA L4 GPUs. The runtime leverages
PagedAttention-based KV-cache management and continuous
batching mechanisms originally proposed for efficient LLM
serving. The GPU inference worker loads the Qwen1.5-1.8B-
Chat model using FP16 precision for optimized inference
throughput. Model execution was performed using the Py-
Torch deep learning framework operating in FP16 precision
mode through the vLLM inference runtime environment [13].
The inference runtime leverages several vLLM optimizations
including continuous batching, KV-cache management, opti-
mized tensor execution, and GPU memory-aware scheduling.
Inference requests are executed using configurable sampling
parameters including temperature and maximum token gener-
ation limits.
I. Runtime Metrics Collection
Runtime metrics are collected throughout the inference
lifecycle to evaluate scheduling behavior under contention.


--- Page 8 ---
TABLE II: Experimental Platform Configuration

Metrics Storage

GPU Monitoring Inter-
val
200 ms
Metrics include queue wait time, inference latency, end-to-
end latency, observed output length, GPU memory utilization,
GPU utilization, throughput, and scheduling fairness.
Worker execution timestamps are recorded before and after
GPU execution to separate queueing latency from inference
runtime latency. Metrics are persisted into CSV files for post-
processing and visualization.
GPU telemetry collection is performed using nvidia-smi
sampling at 200 millisecond intervals during experimental
execution.
J. Adaptive Runtime Token Drift Compensation
A key contribution of DriftSched is its adaptive runtime
token drift compensation mechanism. During inference exe-
cution, the observed output length is compared against the
originally estimated token budget. Deviations between pre-
dicted and observed token generation behavior are treated as
runtime token drift and are used to continuously refine future
workload estimation decisions. The runtime analyzer updates
workload estimation bias using exponential moving average
(EMA)-based correction.
The adaptive update rule is computed as:
Bnew = (1 −α) × Bold + α × Bmeasured
(5)
where:
Bmeasured = Tactual
Tbase
(6)
where Bnew represents the updated runtime bias, Bold
represents the previous bias value, α represents the exponen-
tial moving average learning rate, Bmeasured represents the
observed runtime bias, Tactual represents the observed output
length, and Tbase represents the baseline estimated token count
for the workload category.
Runtime token drift arises when the observed output length
deviates from the workload-specific estimate used during
admission-time scheduling. Since scheduling decisions depend
on predicted workload size, estimation errors may propagate
into queue assignment, workload classification, and scheduling
policy behavior. DriftSched compensates for this effect by
continuously updating workload-specific bias factors using
runtime observations collected after inference completion.
Unlike static token-budget estimation approaches, the pro-
posed framework performs online adaptation throughout exe-
cution. As additional workload observations become available,
the adaptive estimator gradually converges toward workload-
specific operating regions, reducing prediction error and im-
proving scheduling stability under sustained multi-tenant in-
ference traffic.
This adaptive mechanism enables the estimator to com-
pensate for runtime token drift, improve workload prediction
accuracy over time, reduce workload misclassification, and
support more stable admission-time scheduling decisions un-
der heterogeneous multi-tenant inference traffic.
III. EXPERIMENTAL SETUP
A. Experimental Environment
Experiments were conducted on NVIDIA L4 GPU hardware
using vLLM-based inference serving under Ubuntu Linux. Re-
dis was used for distributed queue management and scheduling
coordination. Concurrent workload generation was performed
using Python-based request injection with configurable con-
currency levels to emulate GPU saturation conditions.
The experimental framework supports reproducible schedul-
ing evaluation across multiple scheduling algorithms, work-
load distributions, and concurrency configurations.
The complete DriftSched implementation, experimental au-
tomation scripts, runtime metrics pipeline, workload corpus,
and scheduling framework are publicly available for repro-
ducibility and future research extensions [22].
B. Experimental Configuration
The experimental configuration consisted of 3000 inference
requests, a concurrency level of 50 clients, and three indepen-
dent experimental runs. Experiments were conducted using a
GPU batch size of 32 requests and a batch wait interval of
0.01 seconds before dispatching requests to the GPU inference
worker.


--- Page 9 ---
(a) FIFO
(b) Priority
(c) Weighted
(d) SJF
(e) Aging Priority
Fig. 4: Relationship between semantic workload categories and runtime scheduling classes across all evaluated scheduling
policies. The workload analysis layer first identifies semantic workload categories (short_qa, summary, technical,
and report) and subsequently assigns runtime scheduling classes (short, medium, and long) based on adaptive token-
budget estimation. Results demonstrate that semantic workload labels do not always correspond directly to runtime execution
cost. Report-generation workloads exhibit both medium and long runtime classifications, while technical and summarization
workloads are predominantly classified as medium jobs. These observations highlight the importance of adaptive workload
estimation and motivate the need for runtime token drift compensation prior to admission-time scheduling decisions.
To evaluate the impact of adaptive runtime token drift
compensation, two configurations were tested. Baseline mea-
surements were collected with adaptive bias correction dis-
abled (BIAS=OFF), representing static token-budget estima-
tion without runtime feedback. DriftSched evaluations were
performed with adaptive bias correction enabled (BIAS=ON),
allowing workload-specific bias factors to be continuously
updated using runtime token-generation observations.
Unless otherwise stated, all scheduling comparisons re-
ported in this paper use the BIAS=ON configuration, while
BIAS=OFF results are presented specifically to quantify the
impact of runtime token drift compensation on workload
estimation accuracy.
C. Experimental Hardware and Software Environment
Experiments were conducted on a bare-metal NVIDIA L4
inference platform to evaluate adaptive QoS-aware scheduling
behavior under concurrent multi-tenant LLM inference work-
loads. The experimental environment was designed to emulate
realistic GPU contention scenarios commonly observed in
enterprise AI serving infrastructure. All scheduling policies,
workload generation, runtime telemetry collection, and infer-
ence execution were performed on the same system to ensure
consistent runtime measurements across experiments.
The hardware platform consisted of an NVIDIA L4 infer-
ence accelerator [4] deployed on an Intel Xeon 6 (Granite
Rapids) server platform with DDR5 memory and NVMe-
based storage. GPU telemetry including utilization, memory
consumption, power draw, temperature, and clock frequencies
was collected using NVIDIA-SMI during workload execution.
The complete experimental platform configuration is summa-
rized in Table II. Hardware specifications are presented in
Table IIa, while the software environment configuration is
shown in Table IIb.
IV. RESULTS AND ANALYSIS
A. Experimental Overview
Experiments were conducted on an NVIDIA L4 GPU
using the vLLM inference runtime. A total of 3000 inference
requests were generated across Premium, Standard, and Batch
tenants. Workloads consisted of short question-answering,
summarization, technical explanation, and long-form report
generation tasks. Five scheduling policies were evaluated:
FIFO, Priority Scheduling, Shortest Job First (SJF), Weighted
Scheduling, and Aging Priority Scheduling.
The workload generation process included an initial cali-
bration phase followed by a sustained stress phase. During
the calibration phase, runtime feedback was collected to esti-
mate token-generation drift and update workload-specific bias
factors. The stress phase evaluated scheduler behavior under
high request volume using the learned estimates.


--- Page 10 ---
(a) FIFO
(b) Priority
(c) Weighted
(d) SJF
(e) Aging Priority
Fig. 5: Adaptive bias convergence for FIFO, Priority, Weighted, SJF, and Aging Priority scheduling policies during DriftSched
execution. All workload categories were initialized with a bias value of 1.0 and continuously updated using runtime feedback
collected after inference completion. The vertical dashed line indicates the transition from the calibration phase to the sustained
stress phase. Across all schedulers, bias values rapidly converge from the initial estimate toward stable operating regions between
approximately 0.79 and 0.84. The observed convergence demonstrates the presence of runtime token drift and validates the
effectiveness of the adaptive feedback mechanism in learning systematic differences between estimated and actual token
generation behavior. Despite increased request pressure after the start of the stress phase, learned bias values remain relatively
stable, indicating robust runtime adaptation under sustained multi-tenant GPU inference workloads.
B. Semantic Workloads versus Runtime Scheduling Classes
Figure 4 illustrates the relationship between semantic work-
load categories and runtime scheduling classes across all
evaluated scheduling policies.
The results demonstrate that semantic workload type does
not always correspond directly to runtime execution cost.
While short question-answering workloads were consistently
classified as short jobs and summarization workloads were
predominantly classified as medium jobs, report-generation
workloads exhibited a mixture of medium and long runtime
classifications. This behavior indicates that semantically simi-
lar requests may generate substantially different token counts
during execution.
The observed distribution highlights the limitations of static
workload classification approaches that rely solely on semantic
labels. Assigning all report-generation requests to a single
scheduling category would fail to capture workload variability
and may result in inaccurate scheduling decisions.
DriftSched addresses this challenge by separating semantic
workload classification from runtime scheduling classifica-
tion. The workload analysis layer first identifies the semantic
workload category and then applies adaptive token-budget
estimation to determine the expected runtime execution cost.
Scheduling decisions are therefore driven by estimated infer-
ence cost rather than semantic workload labels alone.
Similar classification distributions were observed across
FIFO, Priority, Weighted, SJF, and Aging Priority scheduling
policies, demonstrating that runtime workload characterization
remains largely independent of the selected scheduling algo-
rithm. These results further support the existence of runtime
token drift and motivate the need for adaptive workload esti-
mation before admission-time scheduling decisions are made.
C. Adaptive Bias Convergence
Figure 5 illustrates the evolution of workload-specific bias
values during execution.
The results show a rapid correction phase during the initial
requests, where bias values quickly adjust from the default
estimate of 1.0 and converge toward stable operating regions.
Across all evaluated scheduling policies, the learned bias
values stabilized between approximately 0.79 and 0.84 across
all workload categories, indicating that static token-budget
estimates consistently overestimated actual token generation
behavior.
The similarity of the learned bias ranges across scheduling
policies is expected because runtime token drift is primar-
ily driven by workload generation characteristics and model
output behavior rather than queue ordering decisions. While
scheduling policies influence queue dynamics, latency, and
fairness, adaptive bias values are learned directly from the
relationship between estimated and observed output lengths
observed during inference execution.
The convergence behavior demonstrates the presence of
systematic runtime token drift and highlights the effectiveness
of adaptive feedback-driven estimation. Despite increasing


--- Page 11 ---
workload pressure after the start of the stress phase, bias values
remained stable across all scheduling policies, indicating that
the adaptive estimator can continuously refine workload char-
acterization under sustained multi-tenant inference workloads.
Experiments were conducted using an expanded work-
load corpus containing approximately 1180 unique prompts
spanning short question-answering, summarization, technical
explanation, and report-generation tasks. The observed conver-
gence therefore suggests that the adaptive estimator is learning
general runtime generation behavior rather than memorizing a
small set of repeated prompts.
D. Tenant Queue Dynamics Under Contention
Figure 6 illustrates queue depth evolution for Premium,
Standard, and Batch tenants across all evaluated scheduling
policies. The red curve represents total queue depth, while
individual tenant queues are shown separately.
The queue depth plots exhibit two distinct buildup phases
that correspond directly to the experimental workflow. The
first queue-growth period occurs during the calibration phase,
where the initial 1000 requests are used to collect runtime
feedback and learn workload-specific bias values for adaptive
token-drift compensation. During this phase, requests accu-
mulate while the estimator converges toward stable workload-
specific operating regions. The second and substantially larger
queue-growth period marks the beginning of the stress evalu-
ation phase. After calibration completes, the remaining 2000
requests are submitted using the learned bias values obtained
during calibration. Because request arrival rates exceed in-
stantaneous GPU processing capacity, queue depth increases
rapidly across all tenant classes, creating a sustained con-
tention scenario suitable for evaluating scheduler behavior
under multi-tenant GPU saturation. The transition between
these two phases is visible as the second queue-depth spike
observed across all scheduling policies.
The results reveal substantial differences in queue manage-
ment behavior under sustained GPU contention. FIFO schedul-
ing exhibits nearly identical queue growth and drain patterns
across all tenant classes, providing fairness but no service
differentiation. In contrast, Priority Scheduling aggressively
favors Premium workloads, causing high-priority queues to
drain rapidly while lower-priority Batch workloads remain
queued for longer periods.
Weighted Scheduling provides a more balanced allocation
strategy. Although Premium tenants continue to receive prefer-
ential treatment, all tenant queues make continuous progress,
reducing starvation risk while preserving QoS differentiation.
Aging Priority Scheduling further improves fairness by gradu-
ally increasing the priority of long-waiting requests, allowing
Batch workloads to eventually execute even under sustained
high-priority traffic.
SJF demonstrates the most aggressive queue reduction be-
havior due to its preference for shorter estimated workloads.
The total queue depth decreases more rapidly than in FIFO
and Priority Scheduling, indicating improved queue efficiency.
Because SJF relies directly on estimated workload size, its
effectiveness is strongly influenced by the accuracy of the
adaptive token estimation layer.
Across all schedulers, the stress phase produces a substantial
increase in queue depth as request arrival rates exceed instan-
taneous GPU processing capacity. The resulting queue buildup
provides a realistic evaluation environment for studying sched-
uler behavior under multi-tenant GPU saturation conditions.
E. Tail Latency Analysis
Figure 7 compares end-to-end latency percentiles across all
evaluated scheduling policies. Tail-latency behavior is particu-
larly important in large-scale serving systems because a small
number of delayed requests can significantly impact overall
user experience. Dean and Barroso demonstrated that tail
latency amplification becomes increasingly severe as system
scale and concurrency increase [8]. Results are reported using
the median (P50), tail (P95), and extreme-tail (P99) latency
metrics.
FIFO, Priority, Weighted, and Aging Priority scheduling
exhibit broadly similar latency behavior, with P95 latency
values near 600 seconds and P99 latency values exceeding 620
seconds. Although Priority and Weighted scheduling provide
service differentiation across tenant classes, their impact on
overall tail latency remains limited under sustained GPU
contention.
SJF achieves the lowest latency across all reported per-
centiles. Compared with FIFO scheduling, SJF reduces median
(P50) latency by approximately 42%, while reducing P95 and
P99 latency by approximately 17% and 16%, respectively. This
improvement results from prioritizing shorter workloads and
reducing queue occupancy for latency-sensitive requests.
The observed latency reduction highlights the importance
of accurate workload-size estimation. Since SJF schedul-
ing decisions depend directly on estimated token budgets,
improvements in runtime token drift compensation lead to
more representative workload classification and more effective
scheduling decisions. These results demonstrate that adaptive
workload estimation can indirectly improve QoS by enabling
schedulers to make more informed admission-time decisions
under GPU saturation conditions.
Across all schedulers, the gap between P50 and P99 latency
remains substantial, indicating that queueing effects dominate
overall response time under sustained multi-tenant load. Con-
sequently, tail-latency reduction remains a critical objective for
future adaptive GPU inference scheduling systems.
TABLE III: Tail Latency Comparison Across Scheduling Poli-
cies (3-Run Average)
Scheduler
P95
σ95
P99
σ99
FIFO
592.957
6.686
630.205
1.502
Priority

1.738

1.792

2.465
631.305
2.715
SJF
491.480
3.995
526.363
5.028
Aging

2.472
644.645
4.905
The tail latency results are summarized in Table III. SJF
achieved the lowest P95 and P99 latency across all evaluated


--- Page 12 ---
(a) FIFO
(b) Priority
(c) Weighted
(d) SJF
(e) Aging Priority
Fig. 6: Tenant queue depth evolution for FIFO, Priority, Weighted, SJF, and Aging Priority scheduling policies during DriftSched
execution. Each subplot shows the Premium, Standard, Batch, and total queue depth over time under the same multi-tenant
request workload. FIFO drains tenant queues uniformly, while Priority and SJF reduce higher-priority or shorter jobs earlier
but allow lower-priority queues to remain longer. Weighted and Aging Priority provide more balanced queue draining across
tenants, with Aging Priority reducing starvation risk by allowing older lower-priority requests to progress over time. The results
show how different scheduling policies affect queue buildup, fairness, and tenant-level waiting behavior under sustained GPU
inference load.
Fig. 7: End-to-end latency comparison across scheduling poli-
cies.
scheduling policies, reducing P99 latency to 526.4 seconds
compared with approximately 630–645 seconds for the re-
maining schedulers. Relative to FIFO, SJF reduced P95 latency
by approximately 17% and P99 latency by approximately
16%, demonstrating the effectiveness of workload-size-aware
scheduling under sustained GPU contention.
This behavior is expected because SJF prioritizes shorter
requests, reducing queue residence time for latency-sensitive
workloads and improving overall latency efficiency. FIFO, Pri-
ority, and Weighted Scheduling produced similar tail-latency
behavior, with P99 latency remaining near 630 seconds. Aging
Priority exhibited the highest P95 and P99 latency because its
fairness-oriented promotion mechanism periodically elevates
long-waiting requests, increasing tail-latency variability.
The relatively small standard deviations observed across
all schedulers indicate stable behavior across repeated experi-
mental runs. Overall, these results demonstrate that workload-
aware scheduling can significantly reduce tail latency under
sustained multi-tenant GPU inference workloads, although the
resulting improvements may come at the expense of tenant-
level QoS guarantees.
TABLE IV: Aggregated Scheduler Performance Across Three
Runs
Scheduler
Avg Queue Wait (s)
P50 (s)
P95 (s)
P99 (s)
FIFO
238.8
184.7

630.2
Priority

Aging
245.0

612.0
644.6
SJF
149.5
106.9
491.5
526.4
Table IV summarizes the aggregated performance of all
evaluated scheduling policies across three independent experi-
mental runs. The results show that scheduling policy selection
has a significant impact on queue waiting time and tail-latency
behavior under sustained multi-tenant GPU contention.
Among all evaluated schedulers, SJF achieves the best
overall performance, reducing average queue waiting time to
149.5 seconds compared with approximately 239–245 seconds
for the other scheduling policies. SJF also produces the
lowest latency across all reported percentiles, reducing median
(P50) latency by approximately 42% relative to FIFO while


--- Page 13 ---
(a) FIFO without adaptive runtime token drift compensation
(b) FIFO with adaptive runtime token drift compensation
Fig. 8: Estimated token budgets versus observed output lengths under FIFO scheduling with BIAS=OFF and BIAS=ON.
Under BIAS=OFF, token estimates remain based on static workload assumptions and exhibit larger deviations from observed
generation behavior. Under BIAS=ON, workload-specific bias correction progressively adjusts estimated token budgets toward
observed output lengths, reducing estimation error and improving admission-time workload classification accuracy over time.
lowering P95 and P99 latency by approximately 17% and 16%,
respectively.
FIFO, Priority, Weighted, and Aging Priority scheduling ex-
hibit broadly similar overall latency characteristics. Although
Priority, Weighted, and Aging Priority introduce tenant-aware
scheduling behavior, their impact on aggregate system latency
remains limited. This observation suggests that tenant-priority
assignment primarily redistributes service quality among ten-
ant classes rather than reducing overall queueing delay.
The results further indicate that workload-aware scheduling
has a greater influence on end-to-end performance than tenant-
priority assignment alone. Because SJF prioritizes requests
using estimated workload size, improvements in adaptive
workload estimation directly translate into improved queue
efficiency and lower latency. These findings highlight the
importance of accurate runtime workload characterization for
reducing queue buildup and improving overall system respon-
siveness under GPU saturation conditions.
Although SJF achieves the best aggregate latency and
queue-wait performance, Table V shows that tenant-aware
schedulers such as Priority, Weighted, and Aging Priority
Scheduling provide substantially stronger QoS differentiation
for Premium tenants. Consequently, the results reveal a fun-
damental tradeoff between global latency optimization and
tenant-level service guarantees in multi-tenant GPU inference
environments.
Table V summarizes average tenant-level latency and queue
waiting behavior across three independent experimental runs
for Premium, Standard, and Batch service tiers. FIFO schedul-
ing produces nearly identical latency and queue wait times
across all tenant classes, demonstrating strong fairness but
providing no explicit QoS differentiation.
Priority Scheduling exhibits the strongest tenant-level ser-
vice differentiation. Premium tenant latency is reduced to ap-
proximately 77 seconds, while Batch tenant latency increases
to approximately 427 seconds. This behavior demonstrates that
strict priority scheduling protects latency-sensitive Premium
workloads at the expense of lower-priority Batch workloads.
TABLE V: Average Tenant-Level End-to-End Latency and
Queue Wait Across Three Experimental Runs

Aging

433.00
422.87
Aging Priority Scheduling exhibits similar behavior, reducing
Premium tenant latency to approximately 76 seconds while
increasing Batch tenant latency to approximately 433 seconds.
These results indicate that both approaches provide strong QoS
guarantees for high-priority tenants under contention.
Weighted Scheduling provides a more balanced allocation
strategy, allowing Premium workloads to receive preferential
treatment while still permitting Standard and Batch tenants to
make continuous execution progress. Premium tenant latency
is reduced to approximately 158 seconds, while Batch tenant
latency remains substantially lower than under strict Priority
and Aging scheduling. As a result, Weighted Scheduling
offers a practical compromise between fairness and service
differentiation.
SJF demonstrates fundamentally different behavior because
scheduling decisions are driven primarily by estimated work-
load size rather than tenant class. Batch workloads experience
the lowest average latency (approximately 95 seconds), while
Premium workloads experience the highest average latency
(approximately 227 seconds). This result indicates that SJF op-


--- Page 14 ---
timizes workload-level efficiency rather than enforcing tenant-
level QoS priorities. Consequently, tenant latency under SJF
is influenced more heavily by workload composition than by
service-tier assignment.
Overall, the results highlight the tradeoff between fair-
ness, latency optimization, and tenant-aware QoS enforce-
ment. FIFO maximizes fairness, Priority and Aging maximize
Premium tenant protection, Weighted Scheduling balances
fairness and service differentiation, and SJF minimizes overall
latency by favoring shorter workloads regardless of tenant
class.
Figure 6 further illustrates these behaviors through tenant-
level queue buildup and drain patterns observed during the
experiment. FIFO maintains similar queue evolution across all
tenant classes, whereas Priority, Weighted, and Aging Priority
exhibit clear service differentiation. SJF displays queue behav-
ior that is primarily determined by workload size rather than
tenant category.
F. Queue Waiting Time by Runtime Workload Class
TABLE VI: Average Queue Waiting Time by Runtime Work-
load Class Across Three Experimental Runs
Scheduler
Short (s)
Medium (s)
Long (s)
FIFO
166.89
258.21
258.04
Priority
168.64
276.74
81.20
Weighted
168.05
265.49
164.95
SJF
2.87
163.18
396.59
Aging
168.65
282.63
83.83
Table VI summarizes average queue waiting time by run-
time workload class across three independent experimental
runs. FIFO scheduling exhibits the expected behavior in which
short workloads experience lower queue delay than medium
and long workloads, while maintaining relatively balanced
treatment across workload classes.
SJF produces the strongest workload-size differentiation.
Short jobs wait only 2.87 seconds on average, while long
jobs wait 396.59 seconds. This confirms that SJF aggressively
prioritizes smaller estimated workloads and shifts queueing
overhead toward larger jobs. The resulting behavior minimizes
latency for short requests but significantly increases waiting
time for long-running workloads.
Priority and Aging Priority exhibit a different pattern be-
cause tenant priority influences queue ordering more strongly
than runtime workload size. Under both policies, long jobs ex-
perience substantially lower queue waiting times than medium
jobs. This occurs because high-priority tenant requests can
be promoted ahead of lower-priority requests regardless of
workload size, demonstrating that tenant QoS enforcement
dominates workload-size optimization.
Weighted Scheduling provides a compromise between
workload-size awareness and tenant-level prioritization. Queue
waiting times remain differentiated across workload classes,
but the degree of separation is less extreme than under SJF or
strict priority-based scheduling.
These
results
highlight
the
importance
of
accurate
workload-size estimation. Because SJF and workload-aware
scheduling decisions depend directly on estimated token bud-
gets, runtime token drift compensation improves queue or-
dering accuracy and helps align scheduling decisions with
actual workload behavior. Consequently, adaptive workload
estimation contributes to improved latency characteristics and
more effective resource utilization under multi-tenant GPU
contention.
G. Impact of Runtime Token Drift Compensation
Figure 8 presents a representative example using FIFO
scheduling, comparing estimated token budgets against ob-
served output lengths under BIAS=OFF and BIAS=ON con-
figurations. Similar convergence behavior and estimation-error
reduction were observed across all evaluated scheduling poli-
cies.
Under BIAS=OFF, the estimator relies on static workload
assumptions and consistently overestimates inference cost
across multiple workload categories. This behavior is visible
as a persistent gap between estimated and observed output
lengths throughout execution.
Under BIAS=ON, workload-specific bias values are con-
tinuously updated using execution feedback. As additional
runtime observations become available, estimated token bud-
gets gradually converge toward observed output behavior.
The reduction in estimation error demonstrates the ability of
DriftSched to compensate for runtime token drift and improve
workload estimation accuracy.
The effect is particularly important for scheduling policies
such as Shortest-Job-First (SJF), where scheduling decisions
depend directly on estimated workload size. Improved estima-
tion accuracy enables more representative workload classifica-
tion and more informed admission-time scheduling decisions
under multi-tenant GPU contention.
Across FIFO, Priority, Weighted, SJF, and Aging Priority
scheduling policies, adaptive bias correction progressively
adjusts workload-specific bias values, improving estimation
stability and reducing classification error over time. These re-
sults demonstrate that runtime token drift is a measurable phe-
nomenon and that feedback-driven bias correction improves
workload estimation accuracy during sustained execution.
Table VII quantifies the estimation accuracy improvements
achieved by adaptive runtime token drift compensation across
all evaluated scheduling policies.
TABLE VII: Average Estimation Error Reduction Across
Three Experimental Runs
Scheduler
MAE Reduction
RMSE Reduction
FIFO
39.51%
41.40%
Priority
39.62%
41.36%
Weighted
38.33%
41.10%
SJF
36.82%
37.18%
Aging
39.74%
41.40%
Average
38.80%
40.49%


--- Page 15 ---
Fig. 9: GPU latency comparison across scheduling policies.
The consistent reduction in MAE and RMSE across all
evaluated scheduling policies indicates that runtime token drift
compensation improves workload estimation independently of
the underlying scheduling algorithm.
Adaptive runtime token drift compensation consistently
reduced workload estimation error across all schedulers. Av-
eraged across three independent experimental runs, MAE
was reduced by approximately 39% and RMSE by approx-
imately 40%. Similar improvements were observed across
FIFO, Priority, Weighted, SJF, and Aging scheduling policies,
demonstrating that the adaptive estimator generalizes across
diverse scheduling strategies and workload conditions. The
observed reductions remained stable despite the use of an
expanded workload corpus containing approximately 1180
unique prompts spanning short question-answering, summa-
rization, technical explanation, and report-generation tasks.
These results demonstrate that runtime feedback enables
substantially more accurate workload characterization than
static token-budget estimation and provides a stronger basis
for admission-time workload classification and scheduling
decisions under multi-tenant GPU contention.
H. Scheduler Comparison
Figure 7 compares end-to-end latency percentiles across all
evaluated scheduling policies.
Among all schedulers, SJF achieves the lowest latency
across P50, P95, and P99 metrics. Compared with FIFO
scheduling, SJF reduces median latency by approximately
42% while also lowering P95 and P99 latency by more than
15%. These improvements result from prioritizing smaller
workloads and reducing queue occupancy for latency-sensitive
requests.
Priority, Weighted, and Aging Priority scheduling provide
tenant-aware service differentiation but exhibit latency char-
acteristics similar to FIFO under sustained GPU contention.
Although these policies improve tenant-level QoS, they do not
significantly reduce overall tail latency.
The results demonstrate that workload-aware scheduling
policies benefit substantially from accurate runtime cost es-
timation. Since SJF scheduling decisions depend directly on
estimated workload size, adaptive runtime token drift compen-
sation enables more representative workload classification and
improves scheduler effectiveness under multi-tenant load.
I. GPU Resource Utilization Analysis
To determine whether scheduler-dependent performance dif-
ferences originate from GPU execution efficiency or queue-
management behavior, GPU memory consumption and GPU
utilization were analyzed across all evaluated scheduling poli-
cies.
Results showed that GPU memory consumption remained
relatively constant throughout execution, stabilizing near 14.5
GB across FIFO, Priority, Weighted, SJF, and Aging Priority
scheduling. This behavior is expected because all experiments
used the same model, inference runtime, and continuous
batching configuration.
Similarly, GPU utilization remained consistently high across
all scheduling policies, typically operating between 85% and
92% during sustained execution. Although minor fluctuations
were observed due to workload arrival patterns and scheduling
decisions, no scheduler produced a substantial change in
overall GPU utilization.
These findings indicate that the latency and queue-wait
differences observed throughout the study are primarily at-
tributable to workload ordering, queue management, and
admission-time scheduling decisions rather than differences in
GPU execution efficiency or memory utilization.
J. GPU Execution Latency
Figure 9 compares GPU inference latency percentiles across
all evaluated scheduling policies. Results are reported using
the median (P50), tail (P95), and extreme-tail (P99) latency
metrics averaged across three experimental runs.
Unlike end-to-end latency measurements, GPU execution
latency remains relatively stable across scheduling policies.
FIFO, Priority, Weighted, and Aging Priority scheduling ex-
hibit nearly identical inference latency distributions, with P50
values near 10.5 seconds and P99 values near 11.3 seconds.
These observations indicate that the underlying GPU execution
cost is largely independent of scheduling policy.
SJF exhibits slightly lower median inference latency while
maintaining similar tail latency characteristics. However, the
magnitude of improvement is significantly smaller than the
reductions observed in end-to-end latency metrics.
The results demonstrate that scheduling policies primarily
influence queue management behavior rather than the compu-
tational cost of model execution. Consequently, the substantial
differences observed in end-to-end latency originate from
workload ordering, queue waiting time, and admission-time
scheduling decisions rather than changes in GPU processing
speed.
This observation highlights the importance of adaptive
workload estimation and queue management in multi-tenant
inference systems. Improvements achieved by DriftSched are
therefore attributable to more effective workload classification
and scheduling decisions rather than modifications to the
underlying inference runtime.


--- Page 16 ---
V. CONCLUSION
This paper presented DriftSched, an adaptive QoS-aware
scheduling framework for multi-tenant LLM inference serving
under runtime token drift. Unlike static scheduling approaches
that rely on fixed workload assumptions, DriftSched continu-
ously compares estimated and observed output lengths and
uses runtime feedback to refine future workload estimation.
Experimental evaluation on NVIDIA L4 GPUs under het-
erogeneous multi-tenant workloads demonstrated that runtime
token drift is a measurable phenomenon that affects workload
classification accuracy and scheduling effectiveness. The pro-
posed adaptive feedback mechanism successfully converged
toward stable runtime operating regions and reduced estima-
tion error over time through continuous runtime feedback.
Results showed that scheduling policy selection significantly
influences queue dynamics, tenant QoS, fairness, and latency
behavior under GPU saturation. While FIFO provided bal-
anced treatment across tenants and Priority-based schedulers
enforced service differentiation, workload-aware scheduling
achieved the largest latency improvements. In particular, SJF
achieved the lowest end-to-end latency across all evaluated
percentile metrics. However, these improvements were ob-
tained by prioritizing workload size rather than tenant class.
As a result, SJF optimizes overall system efficiency and
latency reduction but does not provide explicit tenant-level
QoS guarantees. In contrast, Priority, Weighted, and Aging
Priority scheduling offer stronger service differentiation for
Premium tenants, highlighting the tradeoff between global
latency optimization and tenant-aware fairness.
Analysis further showed that GPU execution latency re-
mained largely unchanged across scheduling policies, indi-
cating that observed performance improvements originated
primarily from improved queue management and workload
estimation rather than changes in model execution cost. These
findings highlight the importance of accurate runtime work-
load characterization for efficient multi-tenant GPU utilization.
Across three independent experimental runs using an
expanded workload corpus containing approximately 1180
unique prompts, adaptive runtime token drift compensation
reduced workload estimation error by approximately 39% in
MAE and 40% in RMSE on average across all evaluated
scheduling policies. These improvements remained consistent
across FIFO, Priority, Weighted, SJF, and Aging schedul-
ing strategies, demonstrating that runtime feedback improves
workload estimation independently of the underlying sched-
uler.
The results suggest that adaptive runtime token drift com-
pensation can serve as a practical enhancement for modern
LLM inference platforms by improving workload estimation,
supporting more informed scheduling decisions, and enabling
better QoS under shared GPU contention. The proposed frame-
work complements existing inference-serving architectures
such as Orca, Sarathi, Nexus, and vLLM by introducing adap-
tive workload estimation as an additional scheduling signal for
multi-tenant GPU environments [16], [17], [19], [20].
The experimental workload corpus consisted of approx-
imately
1180
unique
prompts
spanning
short
question-
answering, summarization, technical explanation, and report-
generation tasks. While this substantially increases workload
diversity and reduces prompt repetition, production AI systems
often experience continuously evolving prompt distributions,
user behavior patterns, and application-specific workload char-
acteristics. In addition, experiments were conducted using a
single GPU platform and a single LLM model configuration.
Consequently, the observed scheduling behavior may vary
under different model architectures, workload distributions,
and multi-GPU deployment scenarios.
This work contributes an adaptive drift-aware scheduling
architecture, a runtime token-drift compensation mechanism,
and a reproducible benchmarking framework for evaluating
QoS-aware LLM inference scheduling on shared GPU infras-
tructure. The findings demonstrate that runtime token drift is
a measurable and practically significant phenomenon in multi-
tenant LLM serving systems, and that adaptive feedback-
driven estimation can substantially improve workload char-
acterization, scheduling effectiveness, and overall QoS under
GPU contention.
Future work will investigate online reclassification of
queued
requests,
adaptive
queue
reordering,
multi-GPU
scheduling, and integration with production LLM serving
frameworks to further improve fairness, utilization, and tail-
latency performance at scale. In addition, reinforcement-
learning-based scheduling approaches may be explored to
dynamically adapt queue assignment, workload prioritization,
and resource allocation decisions based on observed runtime
behavior, workload characteristics, and system utilization pat-
terns [14]. Future work will also replace whitespace-delimited
output-length measurements with tokenizer-exact token ac-
counting to further improve workload-estimation fidelity.
